The below screen shows the active data after clicking Save (activation). Both Product ID (Header) and Order ID (Item) are now successfully generated using late numbering. The Product and its associated Orders are persisted in the database with proper header-item linkage via productid.

Conclusion:

In this blog, we explored how to implement an unmanaged RAP Business Object with key features like Late Numbering, Draft Handling, and Header-Item association. We saw how both Product (Header) and Order (Item) records can be created in draft mode and how their IDs are generated only during the save (activation) phase using late numbering. By handling the logic in the behavior pool and helper class, we achieved full control over data persistence, and associations.

entry.title: Sales Configuration display by CDS with table function/HANA SQL for Embedded Analytics and Fiori RAP
entry.content:
In that blog I want to share approach to display Sales Item Variant Configuration in CDS-way. The approach could be used both in new reporting technology (like Fiori RAP-reporting (managed and unmanaged), Embedded Analytics) and in classical (ALV classic Dynpro, SE16N, SE16H etc.)

Technically, the approach is using the following:

  • Table Function with HANA SQL with scalar and table variable declaration
  • Functions STRING_AGG for row concatenation, CAST for casting to target type, CASE for conditional logic in HANA SQL
  • Window function for grouping and splitting the logic inside SQL-procedure
  • Sorting inside database procedure

So, it can be used both for Sales Item Variant Configuration and as a cheat-sheet for HANA SQL in AMDP-classes.

The blog is dedicated to Natalya Yakimenko; really encouraging and supportive person especially for Analytical Tools in Big Data / HANA.

What is Sales Item Variant Configuration?

In sales order we can parametrize item (configure) with characteristics according to customer needs: width, length, color, chemicals, mechanicals and so on. The configuration – is what the customer wants; so, it could be not only exact values, but interval values also; for example, Carbon (C) between 1 and 2 %. Also, it could be several values: for example, RED, YELLOW color.

On the Figure 1 it is shown sample of exact and multiple values. LENGTH has exact value and Colour has multiple values.

Figure 1 Exact value with UOM for LENGTH and multiple values for Colour

 

On the Figure 2 it is shown interval values in Sales Item Variant Configuration. Carbon (C) has interval from 1 to 2 percents and Hydrogen (H) should be strictly more than 5% (also pay attention to different decimals part on the screenshot).

Figure 2 Sample of Interval values in Sales Item Variant Configuration

if you want more to know about variant configuration - you can check the links:

1) Introducing Variant Configuration ( https://learning.sap.com/courses/variant-configuration-in-sap-s-4hana-enterprise-management/introducing-variant-configuration ) (thanks to @Jelena_Perfiljeva  )

2) Variant Configuration (LO-VC) in SAP S/4HANA ( https://community.sap.com/t5/supply-chain-management-blog-posts-by-members/variant-configuration-lo-vc-in-sap-s-4hana/ba-p/14234012 )

3) Help-documentation on LO-VC component (https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ERP/61155ee7ed304ae8b66b7526555a6a07/1a40b953495bb44ce10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US&version=6.18.latest )

How to read the variant configuration programmatically for reporting purposes?

Classical approach with function

In table VBAP (sales items) there is a special field (VBAP-CUOBJ, or CDS I_SalesDocumentItem-ProductConfiguration ) that links Sales Item Data Model to Variant Configuration Data Model.

For classical reporting very often the function VC_I_GET_CONFIGURATION_IBASE was used. It is still good function, and can be used, however

  • it does not allow us to select by characteristics values
  • it hardly can be used inside new approaches like Embedded Analytics and Fiori RAP-reporting
    DATA lt_CONFIGURATION     TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF conf_out.
    DATA lT_CONF_WITH_AUTHOR  TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF  api_value .

    CALL FUNCTION 'VC_I_GET_CONFIGURATION_IBASE'
      EXPORTING
        instance            = iv_cuobj                 
      TABLES
        configuration       = lt_CONFIGURATION                  
        et_conf_with_author = lT_CONF_WITH_AUTHOR               
      EXCEPTIONS
        instance_not_found  = 1
        OTHERS              = 2.
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      MESSAGE ID sy-msgid TYPE sy-msgty NUMBER sy-msgno
        WITH sy-msgv1 sy-msgv2 sy-msgv3 sy-msgv4 INTO sy-msgli.
    ENDIF.

 

For Embedded Analytics and RAP-applications I decided to create two group of CDS (view entities):

  • Sales Variant Configuration in row-mode with specifying master info about characteristics itself: data type, single/multiple etc.
  • CDS with table function which present any char in the one line.

 

CDS with values in each Row

The CDS (view entity) for Sales Variant Configuration in row-mode with master data is the following. We could see that core tables are: IBIN, IBINVALUES, IBSYMBOL, CABN, CABNT.

The base part: 

define view entity ZI_CUOBJ_CLS300
  as select from ibin       as ibh
    inner join   ibinvalues as ibv    on ibh.in_recno = ibv.in_recno
    inner join   ibsymbol   as ibsymb on ibsymb.symbol_id = ibv.symbol_id
    inner join   cabn       as CABN   on  cabn.atinn = ibsymb.atinn
                                      and cabn.adzhl = '0000'


  association [1] to I_ClfnCharcDesc         as _CharDescr  on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID   = _CharDescr.CharcInternalID
                                                            and _CharDescr.Language           = 'E'
                                                            and _CharDescr.TimeIntervalNumber = '0000'

  association [1] to ZI_CHARVALUE_MD as _CharValueH on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID =  _CharValueH.CHARCINTERNALID
                                                            and $projection.CharcValuestr      =  _CharValueH.CHARCVALUE
                                                            and $projection.CharcValuestr      <> ''

{
  key ibh.instance                      as cuobj,
  key ibh.in_recno                      as IN_RECNO,
  key ibv.in_segmcnt                    as IN_SEGMCNT,

      ibsymb.atinn                      as CHARCINTERNALID,
      cabn.atnam                        as CharHure,
      _CharDescr.CharcDescription       as CharcDescription,
      cabn.atfor                        as dt_type,
      cabn.anzst                        as NumOfChars,
      cabn.anzdz                        as NumOfDecimals,
      cabn.msehi                        as char_uom,
      cabn.atein                        as Single_Mark,
      ibsymb.atcod                      as IntervalType,

      ibsymb.atwrt                      as CharcValuestr,
      ibv.dec_value_from                as CHARCFROMDECIMALVALUE,
      ibv.dec_value_to                  as CHARCUPTODECIMALVALUE,
      ibv.date_from                     as CharcFromDate,
      ibv.date_to                       as CharcUptoDate,
      ibv.time_from                     as CharcFromTime,
      ibv.time_to                       as CharcUptoTime,
      _CharValueH.CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION as CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION,


      /* associations */
      _CharValueH

}

 

Part: ZI_CHARVALUE_MD

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'MasterData for Char Value'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity ZI CHARVALUE_MD
  as select from I_ClfnCharcValue as ValID
  association [0..1] to ZI_CharcValueDesc as _ValDesc     on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID    = _ValDesc.CharcInternalID
                                                                  and ValID.CharcValuePositionNumber = _ValDesc.CharcValuePositionNumber
                                                                  and _ValDesc.Language              = 'E'
                                                                  and _ValDesc.TimeIntervalNumber    = '0000'

  association [1..1] to I_ClfnCharcBasic          as _ClasCharBsc on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID     = _ClasCharBsc.CharcInternalID
                                                                  and _ClasCharBsc.TimeIntervalNumber = '0000'
{
  key ValID.CharcInternalID          as CHARCINTERNALID,
  key ValID.CharcValuePositionNumber as CharValuePos,
      _ClasCharBsc.Characteristic    as CharNamHuRe, --Human Readable
      ValID.CharcValue               as CHARCVALUE,
      _ValDesc.CharcValueDescription as CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION,
      
      ValID.CharcFromDecimalValue    as CHARC_DECVAL,
      
      _ValDesc,
      _ClasCharBsc
}

 

part: ZI_CharcValueDesc

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Characteristcis Value Desc'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity ZI_CharcValueDesc 
    as select from cawnt as CharacteristicValueDesc
  
      association [0..1] to I_Language as _Language 
        on $projection.Language = 'E'
             
{
  key   cast ( CharacteristicValueDesc.atinn as atinn_no_conv preserving type ) as CharcInternalID,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: null
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.atzhl as CharcValuePositionNumber,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: '_Language'
        @Semantics.language: true
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.spras as Language,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: null
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.adzhl as TimeIntervalNumber,
        @Semantics.text: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.atwtb as CharcValueDescription,
        CharacteristicValueDesc.aennr as ChangeNumber,
        @Semantics.businessDate.from: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.datuv as ValidityStartDate,
        @Semantics.businessDate.to: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.datub as ValidityEndDate,
        CharacteristicValueDesc.lkenz as IsDeleted,
        
        _Language
}

 

The result we could see like that (from SE16H).

We could get values, uom (unit of measure), and data type description for each characteristic from sales item variant configuration. We could use it for searching and data validations (during migration for example) also.

 

Figure 3 Sales Item Variant Configuration Sample

It is useful and informative, but it could lead to changes of cardinality in CDS-joins because we could have multiple values for some characteristics, also we need to do cast in each report/application and in case data type of characteristics is changed we need to change application. So, it would be better, for some cases, to get all values from one field. And it is possible with table function, as STRING_AGG is not working in CDS, but either in HANA SQL or ABAP.

 

Intervals could be like that (shown on figure)

 

Figure 4 Different types of intervals in Sales Item Variant Configuration

 

 

CDS with table function / amdp-procedure on HANA SQL

In table function we need do the following:

  • Format values (especially numeric ones) to their format from master data
  • Concatenate values into one field
  • For intervals it is needed to put special symbols: prefix, delimeter and postfix.

 

As in rows-oriented CDS it is using the following tables: VBAP, IBIN, IBINVALUES, IBSYMBOL, CABN, CABNT.

The idea is simple:

  • Target output type is CHAR with length 120 (of course can be longer, but for my purposes it was more than enough). (pay attention to the field ATWRT_STR in the table function below)
  • All values are selecting and casting to the target type depending on decimals in the characteristic’s master data.
  • If there are multiple values – it is concatenated in rows by function STRING_AGG
  • And for output purposes it is concatenated not only with values itself but with special sign (less (<), less or equal (<=), more or equal (=>), more) (pay attention to table declaration _t_interval_extstr and filling) (see figure 2 with Hydrogen value)
  • The procedure returns sorted data – so it can be used in applications for binary search purposes without sorting on the application’s side

 

Code sample for table function for sales variant configuration’s output

@EndUserText.label: 'SOI - Conf Cls300 - AGG mode'
@ClientHandling.type: #CLIENT_INDEPENDENT

define table function ZI_SOI_CLS300_AGG
returns {
  slsh : vbeln_va;
  slsi : posnr_va;
  MATERIAL: matnr;
  Plant: werks_d;
  ItemDescr: arktx;
  PRODUCTCONFIGURATION: cuobj_va;
  
  ORDERQUANTITY: kwmeng;
  OrderQuantityUnit: vrkme;
  
  ATINN : atinn;
  atnam : atnam;
  ATWRT_STR: abap.char(120);
  }
implemented by method zcl_tf_soicls300_agg=>read_cls300_one_line;

 

Code sample for HANA SQL AMPD-procedure

CLASS zcl_tf_soicls300_agg DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.

    CLASS-METHODS read_cls300_one_line
        FOR TABLE FUNCTION ZI_SOI_CLS300_AGG
      .

  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

ENDCLASS.
CLASS zcl_tf_soicls300_agg IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD read_cls300_one_line BY DATABASE FUNCTION
        FOR HDB
        LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
        OPTIONS READ-ONLY
        USING vbap
              ibin
              ibinvalues
              ibsymbol
              cabn
              .


    -- https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud-database/sap-hana-cloud-sap-hana-sqlscript-reference/
    -- ... table-variable-type-definition?locale=en-us

        declare _t_interval_extstr table(
            atcod "$ABAP.type( ibvalcod )",
            pre_fix "$ABAP.type( char2 )",
            delim "$ABAP.type( char5 )",
            post_fix "$ABAP.type( char2 )"
        );

        declare _mandt varchar(3) := session_context('CLIENT');

* see data element  domanin: IBVALCOD
*0   Empty Quantity (No Value Allowed)
*1   = Lower limit
*2   [ Lower limit, upper limit )
*3   [ Lower limit, upper limit ]
*4   ( Lower limit, upper limit )
*5   ( Lower limit, upper limit ]
*6   ( - Infinite, upper limit )
*7   ( - Infinite , upper limit ]
*8   ( Lower limit, infinite )
*9   [ Lower limit, infinite )


        _t_interval_extstr =
            select '1' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, '' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '2' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '3' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '4' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '5' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '6' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '7' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '8' as atcod, '>= ' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '9' as atcod, '>= ' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            ;


          _single_n_intervals =
        with _low_fromto_agg as (

          select
           ibval.mandt,
           ibval.in_recno,
           ibsym.atinn,
           cabn.atnam,

           cast( cabn.atfor as char(70) ) as atwrt,

           case cabn.atfor
              when 'CHAR' then
                 cast(
                    string_agg( ibsym.atwrt, '; ' )
                    as char (120) )

              WHEN 'NUM' then


*    begin of num - case (it consists of two parts: with intervals and strict equal)

               case -- ibsym.atcod
*    no intervals
                when ibsym.atcod = '1' or ibsym.atcod = '8' or ibsym.atcod = '9' then

                 CASE cabn.anzdz

                    when '0' then
                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,0)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '1' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,1)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '2' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,2)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '3' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,3)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '4' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,4)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '5' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,5)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '10' then
                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (21,10)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    ELSE
                         cast( string_agg( ibval.dec_value_from, '+ ' ) as char (120) )

                  END

              else
*    with intervals
                 CASE cabn.anzdz

                   when '0' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,0)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,0)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                   WHEN '1' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,1)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,1)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                   WHEN '2' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,2)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,2)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '3' then
                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,3)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,3)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
                            as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '4' then
                            cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,4)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,4)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )

                     WHEN '5' then

                           cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,5)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,5)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )


                     WHEN '10' then
                           cast(
                              string_agg(
                                concat(
                                    concat(
                                      concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (21,10)) ),
                                      interv.delim
                                      ),
                                    concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (21,10)), interv.post_fix )
                                  )
                                  , '; ' )
                                as CHAR (120) )


                     ELSE
                         cast( string_agg( ibval.dec_value_from, '+ ' ) as char (120) )
                     END

                 END


                END as atwrt_str


              from ibinvalues as ibval
                join ibsymbol as ibsym
             ON ibsym.symbol_id = ibval.symbol_id
             and ibsym.mandt = ibval.mandt

             join cabn as cabn
              on cabn.atinn = ibsym.atinn
              and cabn.adzhl = '0000'
              and cabn.mandt = ibsym.mandt


            join :_t_interval_extstr as interv
                on interv.atcod = ibsym.atcod


           where ibval.mandt = :_mandt

          group by ibval.mandt, ibval.in_recno, ibsym.atinn, ibsym.atcod, cabn.atnam, cabn.atfor, cabn.anzst, cabn.anzdz

        )

        select
          vbap.vbeln as slsh,
          vbap.posnr as slsi,
          vbap.matnr as material,
          vbap.werks as plant,
          vbap.arktx as itemdescr,
         vbap.cuobj as productconfiguration,

         vbap.kwmeng as orderquantity,
         vbap.vrkme as orderquantityunit,

         fromto_agg.atinn,
         fromto_agg.atnam,
         fromto_agg.atwrt_str

            from vbap as vbap

            inner join ibin       as ibh on vbap.cuobj = ibh.instance

            inner join _low_fromto_agg as fromto_agg on fromto_agg.in_recno = ibh.in_recno

            ;

        RETURN
            select * from :_single_n_intervals
            ORDER BY slsh, slsi, atinn
            ;

  eNDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

 

As a result we could see different types of values in one cell but with different formats (with taken into account decimals, multiple values, and interval type)

Figure 5 Result Output of HANA SQL procedure

 

Conclusion

In that blog I have used HANA SQL for data selecting and formatting for Sales Variant Configuration data model. Comments / additions / recommendations are welcomed both as about sales variant configuration and HANA SQL itself. Thank you.

For contacting with me: my linked.

HANA SQL help for aggregate functions could be found here.

entry.title: Build Solutions for SAP Cloud ERP with confidence
entry.content:
Partner Reference Application Family provides Partners with a “golden path” to becoming a SaaS provider of multi-tenant applications based on the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP). The guidance covers building, running, and integrating scalable full-stack cloud applications. Whether you are building a brand-new SaaS product or upgrading an existing one, these resources act as a blueprint for success.

It provides ready-to-run code branches and tutorials to accelerate the development of scalable SaaS solutions. It consists of multiple public SAP-samples repositories on GitHub, offering comprehensive guidance aligned with the “principle of one” and “clean core”. These resources highlight the value of the extensibility environment, demonstrate best practices in code quality and architecture, and serve as demo-ready examples for realistic partner extensions.

CAP Partner Reference Application
A GitHub repository featuring the sample multi-tenant solution “Poetry Slam Manager” built on SAP BTP CAP, along with detailed tutorials for developers and application managers.

When delivering standardized SaaS solutions to multiple customers, customer-specific requirements are inevitable. Using SAP Build and CAP, the solution is extensible out of the box, enabling easy customization. The companion repository, "Partner Reference Application Extension", provides guidance on building and deploying such extensions, including a sample customer-specific extension (“Catering Management”).

ABAP Partner Reference Application

For building ABAP-based side-by-side multi-tenant applications for SAP Cloud ERP, refer to the end-to-end guide. It includes the sample solution “Music Festival Manager” based on ABAP Cloud.

Cross Stack Partner Reference Application
Designed for scenarios requiring on-stack extensions on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition with seamless integration to SAP BTP CAP applications.
This example demonstrates how an on-stack extension integrates with a side-by-side CAP multi-tenant application (Poetry Slam Manager). It includes tutorials to help build and integrate both components, enabling enterprise-grade cross-stack extensions using ABAP Cloud and CAP.

On-Stack Partner Reference Application
Focused on delivering on-stack extensions for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition with scalability, upgrade stability, and seamless integration.
This application integrates with Manage Sales Orders – Version 2 (SAP Fiori app) and showcases event-driven loyalty program management. It demonstrates how standard SAP business processes can be enhanced without modifying the core. Top of Form

 

The Partner Reference Application Family is your accelerator for building next-generation SAP solutions. It’s not just about code—it’s about enabling partners to deliver scalable, extensible, and future-proof applications with speed and quality. By leveraging these ready-made blueprints and best practices, you can reduce development effort, ensure clean core compliance, and focus on what truly matters: delivering value to your customers.

entry.title: Dynamic Field Behavior Using Side Effects
entry.content:
Introduction

In SAP RAP applications, handling dynamic field behavior is a common requirement, especially when UI properties like readonly or mandatory need to change based on user input. Developers often rely on side effects to react to such changes. However, a subtle yet important gap exists,while side effects refresh field values, they do not automatically trigger feature control that governs field behavior. This can lead to confusion when expected UI changes do not occur. In this blog, we will explore this common issue and understand the correct approach to ensure that field behavior is updated dynamically using side effects and permissions.

For example, if the Status can have some data, the Description field should become mandatory and if status doesnot have any data we have to make Description field become read only.

field ( features : instance ) Description;

side effects {
field Status affects field Description;
}

However, this approach only refreshes the field value and does not impact its behavior. As a result, the feature control logic,such as setting a field to readonly, mandatory, or hidden is not triggered. This means that even though the dependent field is refreshed on the UI, its properties remain unchanged, leading to inconsistencies between the expected and actual behavior. Developers may assume that side effects automatically handle both value and behavior updates, but in reality, they only ensure data refresh. To properly influence UI behavior, an additional step is required to explicitly trigger the feature control mechanism.

Solution Overview

  • To ensure that the field behavior is updated correctly, the side effects must explicitly refresh the field permissions instead of just the field value. By doing so, the framework is instructed to re-evaluate the feature control logic, which in turn triggers the feature instance responsible for handling properties like readonly, mandatory, or visibility. This approach ensures that any changes in the dependent field are accurately reflected not only in the data but also in the UI behavior, resulting in a consistent and expected user experience.
  • Whenever Status changes, RAP will re-trigger the feature control logic for the Description field. This ensures that any conditions defined in the feature implementation (for example, making Description readonly when Status = Rejected) are applied immediately on the UI.
side effects {
field Status affects permissions ( field Description );
}

By using permissions, the feature instance is triggered, and the field behavior (such as readonly or mandatory) is updated dynamically as expected.

Interface Behavior Definition

managed implementation in class ZBP_R_TRAV_DBTT unique;
strict ( 2 );
with draft;
extensible;
define behavior for ZR_TRAV_DBTT alias ZrTravDbtt
persistent table ZTRAV_DBTT
extensible
draft table ZTRAV_DBTT_D
etag master LocalLastChangedAt
lock master total etag LastChangedAt
authorization master( global )
{
  field ( mandatory : create )
   TravelID;

  field ( readonly )
   LocalCreatedBy,
   LocalCreatedAt,
   LocalLastChangedBy,
   LocalLastChangedAt,
   LastChangedAt;

  field ( readonly : update )
   TravelID;

   field(features : instance) Description;
   side effects { field Status affects permissions ( field Description);}


  create;
  update;
  delete;

  draft action Activate optimized;
  draft action Discard;
  draft action Edit;
  draft action Resume;
  draft determine action Prepare;

  mapping for ZTRAV_DBTT corresponding extensible
  {
    TravelID = TRAVEL_ID;
    Description = DESCRIPTION;
    Status = STATUS;
    LocalCreatedBy = LOCAL_CREATED_BY;
    LocalCreatedAt = LOCAL_CREATED_AT;
    LocalLastChangedBy = LOCAL_LAST_CHANGED_BY;
    LocalLastChangedAt = LOCAL_LAST_CHANGED_AT;
    LastChangedAt = LAST_CHANGED_AT;
  }

}

Instance Features Implementation

method get_instance_features.

  read entities of zr_trav_dbtt in local mode entity zr_trav_dbtt
    all fields with corresponding #( keys ) result data(lt_travel).

  result = value #( for entity in lt_travel
                    ( %tky = entity-%tky
                      %field-description = cond #( when entity-status is not initial
                                                   then if_abap_behv=>fc-f-mandatory
                                                   else if_abap_behv=>fc-f-read_only ) ) ).


endmethod.


Explanation

  • Reads current entity data (ZR_TRAV_DBTT) using provided keys into LT_TRAVEL
  • Uses IN LOCAL MODE to consider unsaved (buffered) changes
  • Loops through each record to determine field behavior dynamically
  • Maps %TKY to link feature control to the correct instance
  • Applies conditional logic on Status field
  • If Status is filled then Description becomes Mandatory
  • If Status is empty then Description becomes Read-only
  • Populates RESULT to control UI behavior at runtime
  • Works with side effects (affects permissions) to re-trigger feature logic on field change

Preview:

If the status field contains a value, then the description field becomes mandatory.

 

 

If the status field has no value, then the description field is read-only.

 

Realtime Scenario

  • RAP behavior definition to dynamically disable the Approval Status field when the Service Entry Sheet item is already approved. This ensures that once the item reaches a “valid” approved state, the field becomes read-only, preventing any further modifications and maintaining data integrity.
  • You used side effects to automatically trigger a UI refresh whenever the approval status changes. This ensures that as soon as the item is approved, the UI immediately reflects the updated state by disabling the field without requiring a manual refresh, providing a seamless and responsive user experience.

Conclusion

When working with dynamic field behavior in RAP, it is important to understand that standard side effects only refresh field values and do not trigger feature control. To ensure that properties like readonly or mandatory are updated correctly, side effects must be defined using permissions. This approach guarantees that the feature instance is executed and the UI behavior responds accurately to changes in dependent fields.

Resources

SAP HELP DOCUMENTATION: https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-rap/side-effects 

ABAP Development  SAP BTP ABAP environment

entry.title: CL_ABAP_PARALLEL: Parallel Processing Without the aRFC Enable Function Modules
entry.content:
The Problem Every ABAP Developer Knows Too Well

You're running a year-end reconciliation report that loops through 80,000 vendor invoices, validates them against GL postings, and stamps each record with an approval status. In a test system with 500 entries it finishes in under a minute. In production it runs for four hours — and the business is calling.

The knee-jerk fix most developers reach for is raw asynchronous RFC (aRFC): sprinkle CALL FUNCTION … STARTING NEW TASK … DESTINATION IN GROUP, wire up callback handlers, manage semaphore flags, and pray that your RFC server group is configured correctly across all application servers. It works — eventually — but the resulting code is fragile, hard to test, and nearly impossible for the next developer to maintain without a war story attached.

There is a better way: CL_ABAP_PARALLEL.

What Is CL_ABAP_PARALLEL?

CL_ABAP_PARALLEL is a standard SAP class (available from SAP Basis 7.54 and fully released in ABAP Cloud) that wraps the complexity of aRFC-based parallel dispatch behind a clean, object-oriented interface. Instead of orchestrating raw function module calls, RFC groups, and callback patterns yourself, you define what each unit of work should do, hand the instances over to the framework, and let it handle the rest — process allocation, dispatching, timeout management, and result collection.

Under the hood, the framework still uses RS_ABAP_PARALLEL and the server dispatch class CL_SSI_DISPATCH, so the performance characteristics are identical to hand-rolled aRFC. The difference is in developer experience and maintainability.

Two Ways to Implement: Choose Your Pattern

SAP provides two distinct implementation strategies. Pick the one that fits your team's style and the complexity of your data contracts.

Pattern 1 — Interface Implementation (IF_ABAP_PARALLEL)

This is the recommended modern approach, particularly for ABAP Cloud and clean-core projects. Your processing class implements the IF_ABAP_PARALLEL interface and carries its own typed input data as instance attributes. No serialization gymnastics required.

"---------------------------------------------------------------------
" Step 1: Define the processing task class
"---------------------------------------------------------------------
CLASS zcl_invoice_validation_task DEFINITION
  PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_abap_parallel.
    INTERFACES if_serializable_object.

    TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_result,
             invoice_id  TYPE vbeln,
             is_valid    TYPE abap_bool,
             error_msg   TYPE string,
           END OF ty_result.

    METHODS constructor
      IMPORTING
        is_invoice TYPE rbkp.           " MIRO invoice header

    METHODS get_result
      RETURNING VALUE(rs_result) TYPE ty_result.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA ms_invoice TYPE rbkp.
    DATA ms_result  TYPE ty_result.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_invoice_validation_task IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD constructor.
    ms_invoice = is_invoice.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD if_abap_parallel~do.
    " All validation logic lives here — runs in a separate dialog WP
    ms_result-invoice_id = ms_invoice-belnr.

    " Example: Cross-check the invoice amount against the PO tolerance
    DATA(lv_tolerance) = CONV dec15_5( '0.05' ).   " 5 % tolerance
    DATA lv_po_net TYPE wrbtr.

    SELECT SINGLE netwr INTO lv_po_net
      FROM ekko
      WHERE ebeln = ms_invoice-xblnr.  " vendor doc nr maps to PO

    IF lv_po_net IS NOT INITIAL.
      DATA(lv_deviation) = abs( ms_invoice-rwbtr - lv_po_net ) / lv_po_net.
      IF lv_deviation <= lv_tolerance.
        ms_result-is_valid = abap_true.
      ELSE.
        ms_result-is_valid  = abap_false.
        ms_result-error_msg = |Deviation { lv_deviation * 100 } % exceeds tolerance|.
      ENDIF.
    ELSE.
      ms_result-is_valid  = abap_false.
      ms_result-error_msg = 'No matching purchase order found'.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD get_result.
    rs_result = ms_result.
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.


"---------------------------------------------------------------------
" Step 2: Orchestrate the parallel run from your main program / method
"---------------------------------------------------------------------
CLASS zcl_invoice_processor DEFINITION
  PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    CLASS-METHODS run_validation.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_invoice_processor IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD run_validation.

    " Fetch the raw invoice headers to validate
    DATA lt_invoices TYPE TABLE OF rbkp.
    SELECT * FROM rbkp
      INTO TABLE @LT_invoices
      WHERE bukrs = '1000'
        AND bldat >= @( cl_abap_context_info=>get_system_date( ) - 30 ).

    " Build one task object per invoice
    DATA lt_tasks TYPE cl_abap_parallel=>t_in_inst_tab.
    LOOP AT lt_invoices ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<invoice>).
      DATA(lo_task) = NEW zcl_invoice_validation_task( <invoice> ).
      APPEND lo_task TO lt_tasks.
    ENDLOOP.

    " Instantiate the framework — use 40 % of available dialog processes
    DATA(lo_parallel) = NEW cl_abap_parallel(
      p_percentage = 40
      p_timeout    = 300   " seconds per task
    ).

    " Fire!
    DATA lt_results TYPE cl_abap_parallel=>t_out_inst_tab.
    lo_parallel->run_inst(
      EXPORTING p_in_tab  = lt_tasks
      IMPORTING p_out_tab = lt_results
    ).

    " Harvest results
    LOOP AT lt_results ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<res>).

      " Check for framework-level errors first (timeouts, system failures)
      IF <res>-message IS NOT INITIAL.
        MESSAGE <res>-message TYPE 'W'.
        CONTINUE.
      ENDIF.

      " Cast the generic reference back to our concrete task class
      DATA(lo_finished_task) = CAST zcl_invoice_validation_task( <res>-instance ).
      DATA(ls_val_result)    = lo_finished_task->get_result( ).

      IF ls_val_result-is_valid = abap_false.
        " Write rejection log, trigger workflow, etc.
        cl_demo_output=>write( |Invoice { ls_val_result-invoice_id }: { ls_val_result-error_msg }| ).
      ENDIF.

    ENDLOOP.

  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

The key advantage here is type safety. Your invoice data lives in a properly typed class attribute (ms_invoice TYPE rbkp) — no intermediate XSTRING packing or XML transformation. When you cast <res>-instance back to zcl_invoice_validation_task, you get full IntelliSense and compile-time checks in ADT.

Pattern 2 — Inheritance from CL_ABAP_PARALLEL (Classic Style)

In this variant your task class inherits from CL_ABAP_PARALLEL and redefines the DO method. Input and output are exchanged via the inherited P_IN / P_OUT XSTRING parameters, which you serialize and deserialize yourself using EXPORT … TO DATA BUFFER / IMPORT … FROM DATA BUFFER.

This pattern is occasionally useful when working on older Basis releases or when you need to carry a heterogeneous shared payload (P_IN_ALL) across all tasks without repeating it for every record.

CLASS zcl_material_enrichment DEFINITION
  PUBLIC INHERITING FROM cl_abap_parallel
  FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS do REDEFINITION.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_material_enrichment IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD do.
    " Deserialize per-task input
    TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_input,
             matnr TYPE matnr,
             werks TYPE werks_d,
           END OF ty_input.
    DATA ls_input TYPE ty_input.
    IMPORT task_data = ls_input FROM DATA BUFFER p_in.

    " Do the heavy lifting — e.g. call an external pricing API
    DATA lv_price TYPE p LENGTH 10 DECIMALS 2.
    " ... your logic here ...

    " Serialize the result back
    TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_output,
             matnr TYPE matnr,
             price TYPE p LENGTH 10 DECIMALS 2,
           END OF ty_output.
    DATA ls_output TYPE ty_output.
    ls_output-matnr = ls_input-matnr.
    ls_output-price = lv_price.
    EXPORT result_data = ls_output TO DATA BUFFER p_out.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

Practical tip: Prefer Pattern 1 (interface) for all new development. Reserve Pattern 2 for legacy codebases where you need XSTRING-based data exchange with an existing framework or where shared read-only context (P_IN_ALL) can meaningfully reduce payload size.

Constructor Parameters — What They Mean and How They Interact

One of the most common sources of confusion with CL_ABAP_PARALLEL is the interplay between its four resource-control parameters. Here is a clear breakdown:

Parameter Type Effect
P_PERCENTAGEI (0–100)Use X % of the dialog processes that the system deems available at startup. Takes precedence over P_NUM_TASKS if both are supplied.
P_NUM_TASKSIRequest a fixed number of parallel slots. Ignored if P_PERCENTAGE is also set or if the value exceeds the system maximum.
P_NUM_PROCESSESIHard cap on the total simultaneous processes across all app servers. A safeguard against monopolizing the system.
P_TIMEOUTI (seconds)Maximum wall-clock time a single task may run before the framework marks it as failed and populates <res>-message.
P_LOCAL_SERVERFlagWhen set, all tasks run on the same application server as the calling program. Useful for debugging; avoid in production.

Error Handling You Cannot Ignore

Every entry in p_out_tab (or p_out_inst_tab) has a MESSAGE field. This is not optional to check. If a task times out, encounters a system failure, or the framework cannot dispatch it, the result record will have a non-initial MESSAGE and a blank result. Silently skipping that check means silently dropping data.

LOOP AT lt_results ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<r>).
  IF <r>-message IS NOT INITIAL.
    " Log to application log (SLG1) instead of just writing to screen
    cl_bali_log=>...   " or your preferred logging utility
    CONTINUE.
  ENDIF.
  " ... process normal result ...
ENDLOOP.

Additionally, wrap your entire run_inst call in a TRY … CATCH block for cx_sy_parallel_processing to handle catastrophic dispatch failures gracefully.

Quick Comparison: Before and After

Aspect Raw aRFC Pattern CL_ABAP_PARALLEL
BoilerplateRFC function module + callback handler + semaphore flagsOne class implementing IF_ABAP_PARALLEL
Type safetyEXPORT/IMPORT tables, genericTyped class attributes (Pattern 1)
Error handlingManual SYSTEM-EXCEPTIONS per callBuilt-in MESSAGE field per result
TestabilityDifficult (requires RFC infrastructure)Standard class — full unit-test coverage possible
ABAP Cloud compatibleNo (aRFC not available in Cloud)Yes (CL_ABAP_PARALLEL is released)

Closing Thoughts

CL_ABAP_PARALLEL does not do anything fundamentally new — parallel dialog dispatch has been in ABAP for decades. What it does is remove all the ceremony that made developers avoid it. With the interface-based pattern, adding parallelism to an existing data-processing class is often a matter of implementing one interface, one constructor, and adjusting a handful of lines in the orchestrating method.

If your team has been avoiding parallel processing because the old aRFC boilerplate felt too risky or too expensive to maintain, it is time to revisit that decision. Your business users — and the developers who come after you — will thank you.

entry.title: Implementing Chained Actions in Unmanaged SAP RAP
entry.content:
Introduction:

A chain action is when one RAP action calls another action internally using EML: 

Why Chained Actions in Unmanaged RAP?

  • No automatic save handling (you control everything)
  • No framework-managed validations
  • Risk of multiple DB updates / inconsistent state
  • Chaining ensures:
    • Single transactional flow
    • Controlled execution order
    • One final Modify Entities

Architecture Overview: 

UI / EML Call
→ Behaviour Definition (Action Triggered)
→ Behaviour Handler (Main Action Method)
→ Execute Child Action 1 (Validation / Check)
→ Execute Child Action 2 (Preparation / Data Collection)
→ Transactional Buffer (All changes stored in memory)
→ Final MODIFY ENTITIES (Single update)
→ Saver Class (Save Sequence)
→ Database Commit

Implementation: 

  • Step 1: Define Actions In BDEF 
    •  Declare :
      • One Main Action
      • One or More Child Actions

  • Step 2: Implement child Actions in BP class 
METHOD checkBeforeDelivery.

  LOOP AT keys INTO DATA(ls_key).

    READ ENTITIES OF yi_salesorder IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY yi_salesorder
      FIELDS ( Status BillingStatus )
      WITH VALUE #( ( %tky = ls_key-%tky ) )
      RESULT DATA(lt_data).

    READ TABLE lt_data INTO DATA(ls_data) INDEX 1.

    IF ls_data-Status = 'Delivery Created'.

      APPEND VALUE #( %tky = ls_key-%tky ) TO failed-yi_salesorder.

      APPEND VALUE #(
        %tky = ls_key-%tky
        %msg = new_message(
          id       = 'YCHAINED_MESSAGE'
          number   = '001'
          severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
          v1       = 'Delivery already created'
        )
      ) TO reported-yi_salesorder.

    ENDIF.

    IF ls_data-BillingStatus = 'GENERATED'.

      APPEND VALUE #( %tky = ls_key-%tky ) TO failed-yi_salesorder.

      APPEND VALUE #(
        %tky = ls_key-%tky
        %msg = new_message(
          id       = 'YCHAINED_MESSAGE'
          number   = '002'
          severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error
          v1       = 'Bill already generated'
        )
      ) TO reported-yi_salesorder.

    ENDIF.

  ENDLOOP.

ENDMETHOD.
METHOD GenerateBill.

  LOOP AT keys INTO DATA(ls_key).

    APPEND VALUE #(
      %tky = ls_key-%tky
      %param-BillingStatus = 'GENERATED'
    ) TO result.

  ENDLOOP.

ENDMETHOD.
  • Step 3: Implement Main Action 
METHOD createdelivery.

  MODIFY ENTITIES OF yi_salesorder IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY yi_salesorder
    EXECUTE checkBeforeDelivery
    FROM CORRESPONDING #( keys )
    FAILED DATA(lt_v_failed)
    REPORTED DATA(lt_v_reported).

  IF lt_v_failed IS NOT INITIAL.
    failed-yi_salesorder   = CORRESPONDING #( BASE ( failed-yi_salesorder ) lt_v_failed-yi_salesorder ).
    reported-yi_salesorder = CORRESPONDING #( BASE ( reported-yi_salesorder ) lt_v_reported-yi_salesorder ).
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.

  MODIFY ENTITIES OF yi_salesorder IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY yi_salesorder
    EXECUTE GenerateBill
    FROM CORRESPONDING #( keys )
    RESULT DATA(lt_invoice_result_raw)
    FAILED DATA(lt_chain_failed)
    REPORTED DATA(lt_chain_reported).

  failed-yi_salesorder   = CORRESPONDING #( BASE ( failed-yi_salesorder ) lt_chain_failed-yi_salesorder ).
  reported-yi_salesorder = CORRESPONDING #( BASE ( reported-yi_salesorder ) lt_chain_reported-yi_salesorder ).

  IF lt_chain_failed IS NOT INITIAL.
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.

  DATA(lt_billing_result) = lt_invoice_result_raw.

  MODIFY ENTITIES OF yi_salesorder IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY yi_salesorder
    UPDATE FIELDS ( Status BillingStatus )
    WITH VALUE #( FOR key IN keys (
      %tky = key-%tky
      Status = 'Delivery Created'
      BillingStatus = VALUE #(
        lt_billing_result[ %tky = key-%tky ]-%param-BillingStatus OPTIONAL )
      %control-Status        = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
      %control-BillingStatus = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
    ) )
    FAILED DATA(lt_u_failed)
    REPORTED DATA(lt_u_reported).

  failed-yi_salesorder   = CORRESPONDING #( BASE ( failed-yi_salesorder ) lt_u_failed-yi_salesorder ).
  reported-yi_salesorder = CORRESPONDING #( BASE ( reported-yi_salesorder ) lt_u_reported-yi_salesorder ).

  IF lt_u_failed IS INITIAL.

    READ ENTITIES OF yi_salesorder IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY yi_salesorder
      ALL FIELDS WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
      RESULT DATA(lt_result).

    result = VALUE #( FOR ls_res IN lt_result (
      %tky   = ls_res-%tky
      %param = ls_res
    ) ).

  ENDIF.

ENDMETHOD.
  • Step 3.1: Execute First Action 
  MODIFY ENTITIES OF yi_salesorder IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY yi_salesorder
    EXECUTE checkBeforeDelivery
    FROM CORRESPONDING #( keys )
    FAILED DATA(lt_v_failed)
    REPORTED DATA(lt_v_reported).
  • Step 3.2: Stop on Error
  IF lt_v_failed IS NOT INITIAL.
    failed-yi_salesorder   = CORRESPONDING #( BASE ( failed-yi_salesorder ) lt_v_failed-yi_salesorder ).
    reported-yi_salesorder = CORRESPONDING #( BASE ( reported-yi_salesorder ) lt_v_reported-yi_salesorder ).
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.
  • Step 3.3: Execute Second Action
  MODIFY ENTITIES OF yi_salesorder IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY yi_salesorder
    EXECUTE GenerateBill
    FROM CORRESPONDING #( keys )
    RESULT DATA(lt_invoice_result_raw)
    FAILED DATA(lt_chain_failed)
    REPORTED DATA(lt_chain_reported).
  • Step 3.4: Final Update
  MODIFY ENTITIES OF yi_salesorder IN LOCAL MODE
    ENTITY yi_salesorder
    UPDATE FIELDS ( Status BillingStatus )
    WITH VALUE #( FOR key IN keys (
      %tky = key-%tky
      Status = 'Delivery Created'
      BillingStatus = VALUE #(
        lt_billing_result[ %tky = key-%tky ]-%param-BillingStatus OPTIONAL )
      %control-Status        = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
      %control-BillingStatus = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
    ) )
    FAILED DATA(lt_u_failed)
    REPORTED DATA(lt_u_reported).
  • Step 3.5: Return Result to UI 
    READ ENTITIES OF yi_salesorder IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY yi_salesorder
      ALL FIELDS WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
      RESULT DATA(lt_result).

    result = VALUE #( FOR ls_res IN lt_result (
      %tky   = ls_res-%tky
      %param = ls_res
    ) ).
  • Final Flow 

Main Action(create Delivery) 
  ↓ 
EXECUTE action1 (Validation-checkBeforeDelivery) 
  ↓ 
EXECUTE action2 (GenerateBill) 
  ↓ 
Final MODIFY (Update Fields) 
  ↓ 
SAVE → DB 

  • Output:

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

  • Conclusion

Chain actions in RAP provide a clean and structured way to execute multiple steps within a single transactional flow. By orchestrating actions through EML, you can break complex logic into smaller, manageable units while still maintaining consistency. 

The architecture ensures that all steps run inside the same transactional buffer, and only the final state persists through a single update and save sequence. This avoids common issues like buffer overwrites and inconsistent data.

entry.title: Pessimistic Concurrency Control (Locking) using Unmanaged Scenario in RAP
entry.content:
Introduction: 

SAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP), locking has a major impact because if multiple users can update the same record simultaneously, it leads to data inconsistencies or overwrites. To work on this concurrency control, we have two different approaches-  

  1. Pessimistic concurrency control (locking). 
  2. Optimistic concurrency control (Etag or Odata Etag). 

In a RAP unmanaged scenario, pessimistic concurrency control (locking) is particularly useful because it assumes conflicts are likely and prevents them upfront by locking the record as soon as a user starts editing (like delete, update or modify operations) it. In an unmanaged scenario, the RAP framework does not automatically handle locking. Developers are responsible for implementing the entire locking mechanism manually. 

Pessimistic Concurrency Control (Locking): In RAP business objects, enqueue locks are used for pessimistic concurrency control. Exclusive locking happens on data. 

  • Exclusive Locking: Before an operation (like an UPDATE or DELETE) is executed, the RAP framework acquires an exclusive lock on the specific data instance in a global lock table. 
  • Every time a lock is requested, the system checks the lock table. If another user already holds a lock on that specific record, the new request is rejected immediately by throwing an error message. 
  •  The lock is released in the scenarios like, 
  1. ABAP session gets terminated. 
  2.  LUW gets terminated by calling commit or Rollback. 
  3.  Draft scenario is discarded or expired.

Scenario: 

In this application, we have a Sales (Header) table (ZDB_SALES_HDR) and Sales (Item) table (ZDB_SALES_ITEM) linked through so_id. Need to set the actual database lock by explicitly calling the standard Enqueue Object inside the FOR LOCK method. Users should also be able to save incomplete data as draft and complete it later. To handle custom logic and manual persistence, an unmanaged RAP approach is used. 

1. Database tables:

@EndUserText.label : 'Header sales table' 
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE 
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT 
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A 
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED 
define table zdb_sales_hdr { 

key client  : abap.clnt not null; 
key so_id   : abap.char(10) not null; 
customer_id : abap.char(10); 
order_date  : sydatum; 
@Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'zdb_sales_hdr.currency' 
total_amount: abap.curr(10,2); 
currency    : abap.cuky; 
@Semantics.amount.currencyCode : ' zdb_sales_hdr.currency' 
discount_pct: abap.curr(10,2); 
status      : zde_or_status; 
created_by  : syuname; 
created_on  : timestampl; 
approved_by : syuname; 
approved_on : sydatum; 
}

 

@EndUserText.label : 'Item sales table' 
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE 
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT 
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A 
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED 

define table zdb_sales_item { 
key client  : abap.clnt not null; 
key so_id   : abap.char(10) not null; 
item_no     : abap.char(10); 
material_id : abap.char(10); 
@Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure : ' zdb_sales_item.uom' 
quantity    : abap.quan(5,2); 
uom         : meins; 
@Semantics.amount.currencyCode : ' zdb_sales_item.currency' 
price       : abap.curr(10,2); 
currency    : abap.cuky; 
plant       : werks_d; 
}

2. Created draft tables for both Sales (Header) and Sales (Item) entities to support draft functionality. These tables store intermediate data, allowing users to save incomplete records and activate them later.

@EndUserText.label : 'Draft table for entity ZI_HDR_SALES'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #EXTENSIBLE_ANY
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zdb_header_draft {

  key mandt     : mandt not null;
  key soid      : abap.char(10) not null;
  key draftuuid : sdraft_uuid not null;
  customerid    : abap.char(10);
  orderdate     : sydatum;
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'zdb_header_draft.currency'
  totalamount   : abap.curr(10,2);
  currency      : abap.cuky;
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'zdb_header_draft.currency'
  discountpct   : abap.curr(10,2);
  status        : abap.char(10);
  createdby     : syuname;
  createdon     : timestampl;
  approvedby    : syuname;
  approvedon    : sydatum;
  "%admin"      : include sych_bdl_draft_admin_inc;
}

 

@EndUserText.label : 'Draft table for entity ZI_ITEM_SALES'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #EXTENSIBLE_ANY
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zdb_item_draft {

  key mandt       : mandt not null;
  key soid        : abap.char(10) not null;
  key draftuuid   : sdraft_uuid not null;
  parentdraftuuid : sdraft_uuid;
  itemno          : abap.char(10);
  materialid      : abap.char(10);
  @Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure : 'zdb_item_draft.uom'
  quantity        : abap.quan(5,2);
  uom             : meins;
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'zdb_item_draft.currency'
  price           : abap.curr(10,2);
  currency        : abap.cuky;
  plant           : werks_d;
  "%admin"        : include sych_bdl_draft_admin_inc;
}

3. Created an Interface view on top of these two database table. Defined a proper association between Sales (Header) and Sales (Item) using so_id to establish the relationship. 

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK 
@EndUserText.label: 'Interface view for sales header table' 
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true 

define root view entity ZI_HDR_SALES  
as select from zdb_sales_hdr 
composition [1..*] of ZI_ITEM_SALES as _salesitem 
{ 
key so_id    as SoId, 
customer_id  as CustomerId, 
order_date   as OrderDate, 
@Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'Currency' 
total_amount as TotalAmount, 
currency     as Currency, 
@Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'Currency' 
discount_pct as DiscountPct, 
status       as Status, 
created_by   as CreatedBy, 
created_on   as CreatedOn, 
approved_by  as ApprovedBy, 
approved_on  as ApprovedOn, 
_salesitem 
}

 

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE] 
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED 
@EndUserText.label: 'Interface view for sales item table' 
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true 
@ObjectModel.usageType:{ 
serviceQuality: #X, 
sizeCategory: #S, 
dataClass: #MIXED 
} 
define view entity ZI_ITEM_SALES  
as select from zdb_sales_item 
association to parent ZI_HDR_SALES as _header 
on $projection.SoId = _header.SoId 
{ 
key so_id   as SoId, 
item_no     as ItemNo, 
material_id as MaterialId, 
@Semantics.quantity.unitOfMeasure: 'Uom' 
quantity    as Quantity, 
uom         as Uom, 
@Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'Currency' 
price       as Price, 
currency    as Currency, 
plant       as Plant, 
_header 
}

4. Created a consumption CDS view on top of the interface views to expose the data for UI usage. Instead of using metadata extensions, all required UI annotations were directly maintained within the consumption view for simplicity. 

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK 
@EndUserText.label: 'Projection view for sales header table' 
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true 
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true 

define root view entity ZC_HDR_SALES  
provider contract transactional_query 
as projection on ZI_HDR_SALES 
{ 
@UI.facet: [ 
{ position: 10, label: 'Sales Header', purpose: #STANDARD, type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE }, 
{ position: 20, label: 'Sales Item', purpose: #STANDARD, type: #LINEITEM_REFERENCE, targetElement: '_salesitem' } 
] 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 10, label: 'Sales Id' }], 
identification: [{ position: 10, label: 'Sales Id' }] 
} 
SoId; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 20, label: 'Customer Id' }], 
identification: [{ position: 20, label: 'Customer Id' }] 
} 
CustomerId; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 30, label: 'Order date' }], 
identification: [{ position: 30, label: 'Order date' }] 
} 
OrderDate; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 40, label: 'Total amount' }], 
identification: [{ position: 40, label: 'Total Amount' }] 
} 
TotalAmount; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 50, label: 'Currency' }], 
identification: [{ position: 50, label: 'Currency' }] 
} 
Currency; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 60 }], 
identification: [{ position: 60, label: 'Discount price' }] 
} 

@UI.hidden: true 
DiscountPct; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 70, label: 'Order Status' }], 
identification: [{ position: 70, label: 'Status' }] 
} 

@Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{  
entity:{  
name : 'ZI_NK_STATUS_VH', 
element: 'Status' 
} 
}] 
Status; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 80, label: 'Created by' }], 
identification: [{ position: 80, label: 'Created by' }] 
} 
CreatedBy; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 90, label: 'Created on' }], 
identification: [{ position: 90, label: 'Created on' }] 
} 
CreatedOn; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 100 }], 
identification: [{ position: 100, label: 'Approved by' }] 
} 

@UI.hidden: true 
ApprovedBy; 

@UI: { 
lineItem: [{ position: 110 }], 
identification: [{ position: 110, label: 'Approved on' }] 
} 

@UI.hidden: true 
ApprovedOn; 
_salesitem : redirected to composition child ZC_ITEM_SALES 
}

 

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED 
@EndUserText.label: 'Projection view for sales itemtable' 
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true 
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true 

define view entity ZC_ITEM_SALES  
as projection on ZI_ITEM_SALES 
{ 
@UI.facet: [{ position: 10, label: 'Sales Item', purpose: #STANDARD, type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE }] 

@UI:{ 
lineItem: [{ position: 10, label: 'Sales Id' }], 
identification: [{ position: 10, label: 'Sales Id' }] 
} 
SoId; 

@UI:{ 
lineItem: [{ position: 20, label: 'Item no'}], 
identification: [{ position: 20, label: 'Item no' }] 
} 
ItemNo; 

@UI:{ 
lineItem: [{ position: 30, label: 'Material Id' }], 
identification: [{ position: 30, label: 'Material id' }] 
} 
MaterialId; 

@UI:{ 
lineItem: [{ position: 40, label: 'Quantity' }], 
identification: [{ position: 40, label: 'Quantity' }] 
} 
Quantity; 

@UI:{ 
lineItem: [{ position: 50, label: 'Unit' }], 
identification: [{ position: 50, label: 'Unit of measure' }] 
} 

@UI.hidden: true 
Uom; 

@UI:{ 
lineItem: [{ position: 60, label: 'Price'}], 
identification: [{ position: 60, label: 'Price' }] 
} 
Price; 

@UI:{ 
lineItem: [{ position: 70 }], 
identification: [{ position: 70, label: 'Currency' }] 
} 
Currency; 

@UI:{ 
lineItem: [{ position: 80, label: 'Plant'}], 
identification: [{ position: 80, label: 'Plant' }] 
} 
Plant; 
_header : redirected to parent ZC_HDR_SALES 
}

5. Created behavior definitions on top of the Sales (Header) interface view. Defined the required operations and enabled features like draft handling, associations, specified lock master (for the root entity) and lock dependent (for child entity) to tell the framework that locking is required as per our requirements. 

Specify lock master (for the root entity). 

define behavior for ZI_HDR_SALES //alias <alias_name> 
draft table zdb_header_draft 
late numbering 
lock master 
total etag CreatedBy 
authorization master ( instance ) 
{ 
create; 
update ( features : instance ); 
delete ( features : instance ); 
field ( readonly ) SoId; 
}

Specify lock dependent (for child entity). 

define behavior for ZI_ITEM_SALES //alias <alias_name> 
draft table zdb_item_draft 
late numbering 
lock dependent by _header 
authorization dependent by _header 
{ 
update; 
delete; 
field ( readonly ) SoId; 
association _header { with draft; } 
}

5. Created a lock object by providing Sales (Header) as Primary table. 

Selected lock mode as write lock. 

Activated, Generated Enqueue and Dequeue Objects. 

6. Implemented the required business logic in the behavior pool class using a helper class. This includes handling calculations, and manual data persistence for both Sales entities. 

Inside the FOR LOCK method in the behavior pool class, must explicitly call the created lock object( ‘EZLOCK_SALES' ) to set the actual database lock. 

METHOD lock. 
TRY. 
DATA(lock) = cl_abap_lock_object_factory=>get_instance( iv_name = 'EZLOCK_SALES' ). 
CATCH cx_abap_lock_failure INTO DATA(exception). 
RAISE SHORTDUMP exception. 
ENDTRY. 

LOOP AT keys ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_data>). 
TRY. 
lock->enqueue( 
it_parameter = VALUE #( ( name = 'Sales Id' value = REF #( <fs_data>-SoId ) ) ) 
). 
CATCH cx_abap_foreign_lock INTO DATA(foreign_lock). 

APPEND VALUE #( 
SoId = keys[ 1 ]-SoId 
%msg = new_message_with_text( 
severity = if_abap_behv_message=>severity-error 
text = 'Record is locked by ' && foreign_lock->user_name 
) 
) TO reported-zi_hdr_sales. 

APPEND VALUE #( 
SoId = keys[ 1 ]-SoId 
) TO failed-zi_hdr_sales. 
CATCH cx_abap_lock_failure INTO exception. 
RAISE SHORTDUMP exception. 
ENDTRY. 
ENDLOOP. 
ENDMETHOD.

Output: 

If both the users are accessing the same application then, 

  • First user opens the record in edit mode.  
  • Trigger the operation that calls the lock method (e.g., Edit action).  
  • If the second user try to edit the same record.  

System should prevent editing by throwing Error message like: “Record is locked by another user”.  

Conclusion:

Pessimistic concurrency control (locking) in an unmanaged RAP scenario is fundamental to safeguarding business-critical data by preventing concurrent modifications at the source. It shifts control entirely to the developer, making correct lock design essential for ensuring consistency, reliability, and transactional integrity. While it may introduce contention and affect performance if misused, a well-implemented locking strategy delivers predictable system behavior and eliminates costly data conflicts. Ultimately, it is a key pillar for building robust, enterprise-grade RAP applications where data accuracy cannot be compromised.

entry.title: LOOP AT internal_table STEP — Because Life Is Too Short to Write Counter Variables
entry.content:
Simple but annoying.

Imagine the situation like, it is Monday morning. You have a fresh cup of coffee, positive attitude and simple ABAP task: loop through an internal table backwards. In general, sounds easy and when you open code editor then reality hits. Personally, I would be lazy to add some extra counters and start thinking how can I make it more beautiful and easy. Previously you would end-up with something similar as:

DATA(lv_index) = lines( lt_orders ).

WHILE lv_index > 0.
  READ TABLE lt_orders INDEX lv_index INTO DATA(ls_order).
  " ... adding some logic here ...
  lv_index -= 1.
ENDWHILE.

It works. Nobody is arguing that, but looking at the code, something from my heart will question can we make it better and don't even get me started on what happens with hashed table. That's a story for a different blog post and a stronger beverage 😉

Good News: Someone at SAP Was Listening

Starting with ABAP 7.57, a new addition called STEP arrived for LOOP AT. And honestly? It's one of those features that makes you think "Now, it is show time" as Johnny Cage.

The idea is beautifully simple. You tell LOOP AT how many rows to jump at a time, and in which direction. That's it.

LOOP AT lt_orders INTO DATA(ls_order) STEP n.
  " Your logic goes here ...
ENDLOOP.

The value of n  does two things at once:

  • The sign (positive or negative) controls the direction of the loop
  • The absolute value controls the step size (how many rows to skip)

Going Forward, But Make It Sparse

Let's say you have 10 rows and you only want to process every second one. No problem:

LOOP AT lt_orders INTO DATA(ls_order) STEP 2.
  " Processes rows 1, 3, 5, 7, 9
ENDLOOP.

Going Backwards — The Main Event

Now for the exciting part. To loop through a table in reverse order, you just use a negative value:

LOOP AT lt_orders INTO DATA(ls_order) STEP -1.
  " Processes rows: last → first
ENDLOOP.

Mixing STEP with FROM and TO

You can also combine STEP  with FROM and TO to narrow down the range. But here's the part that catches people off guard: when using a negative STEP, FROM must be greater than TO.

" Process rows 8 down to 3, going backwards
LOOP AT lt_orders INTO DATA(ls_order) FROM 8 TO 3 STEP -1.
  " Processes rows: 8, 7, 6, 5, 4, 3
ENDLOOP.

What about sy-tabix? Good question, it behaves exactly as you'd expect, namely it reflects to the actual row number in the table, not just some counter that goes 1, 2, 3 and so on. Besides it works with DELETE statement as well.

That's it. No counter. No WHILE. No READ TABLE. No cold coffee 😊

Quick Reference

What you want to doHow to write it

Process every 2nd row

LOOP AT itab ... STEP 2

Process in reverse order

LOOP AT itab ... STEP -1

Reverse, skip every other

LOOP AT itab ... STEP -2

Reverse within a range

LOOP AT itab ... FROM 8 TO 3 STEP -1

Delete every 3rd row

DELETE itab STEP 3

Happy coding.

P. S. I didn't find blog describing the LOOP AT ... STEP, so If you find something similar, please feel free to share in the comment section. I will be happy to check it as well.

entry.title: RAP - Step-by-Step Guide to Creating an Overview Page Dashboard with Fiori Elements
entry.content:
Overview page is generally used for grouping Embedded Analytics upon different dimension which flows towards individual entities. But, Have you ever wondered on creating overview page with RAP on Backend. This blog will explain on the same in detail. 

Overview for creating Overview page - Dashboard:

We are going with complete end-end cloud ready approach and for the same I've used BTP Cloud foundry Environment for this Dashboard creation. Below are the sequential steps to create overview page Dashboard. 

Backend:

1. Set up BTP Cloud foundry Environment and enable it in Eclipse ADT.

2. Create Individual Entities using RAP Custom Entity/CDS View. 

3. Create Filter Entity which will be enabled as common live Filter

4. Create Business logic class for these Custom Entities.

Front End:

1. Create New Project with Overview Page Template

2. Link the Entities in BAS and create different cards as per the requirement.

3. Dry run the app to debug and check backend and front end is interlinked and fiori element is configured properly. 

Below Flow chart explains the complete process precisely.

Now let's start the Development. 

 Set up BTP Cloud foundry Environment and enable it in Eclipse ADT

1. Login to your BTP Cockpit as below. 

2. Click Boosters from the left-hand navigation pane of your Global Account view and select prepare an Account for ABAP Trail.

3. The system automatically assigns roles, establishes the Cloud Foundry runtime, builds a dev space, and spins up the ABAP service instance.Once complete, skip down to the Generate a Service Key. Download the service key once the process is completed and store it locally.

4. Now navigate to SubAccount, Instances and Subscriptions and locate the ABAP instance. Right click on 3 dots to create service key.

5. Once service key is created, click on it and navigate to the JSON that it shows. Now, copy the JSON and navigate to Eclipse.

6. Now in Eclipse create new project and enter the JSON that has been copied from BTP Cockpit. This JSON will create the cloud project in Eclipse. 

Create Individual Entities using RAP Custom Entity/CDS View

1. Since BTP Cockpit in Trail does not have valid SAP Predefined Tables, Let's create the custom ZTable - ZOVP_ANALYTICS as below.

 

@EndUserText.label : 'Table for analytics'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #ALLOWED
define table zovp_analytics {

  key client_id : abap.clnt not null;
  key orderid   : abap.char(10) not null;
  region        : abap.char(20) not null;
  division      : abap.char(5);
  districhl     : abap.char(5);
  customer      : abap.char(10);
  status        : abap.char(20);
  order_del     : abap.int4;
  tax           : abap.dec(10,3);
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'zovp_analytics.currency'
  totalamount   : abap.curr(15,2);
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'zovp_analytics.currency'
  targetsales   : abap.curr(15,2);
  currency      : abap.cuky;

}

Note: Before getting into next steps, let's have a quick overview on RAP Custom Entity. RAP Custom Entity is built on RAP Query Provider Interface which will collectively carry the logic for the desired Entity Type. This can be directly given as entity in service definition and also can be exposed in service binding.

2. Now for Overview Page Filter, lets create  Separate  RAP Custom Entity with Region as Filter. Note the case sensitive in Filter Entity and Child Entities should be same and with that only $filter will correctly map the appropriate filter while triggering target entities after entering filter value. 

@EndUserText.label: 'Global Filter Entity'
@ObjectModel.query.implementedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_OVP_RAP_QUERY'
define root custom entity ZCE_FilterEntity
{
  @UI.selectionField: [{ position: 10 }]
  @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{ entity: { name: 'ZCE_VH_Region', element: 'Region' } }]
  @EndUserText.label: 'Region'
  key Region      : tcode;
}

3. Let’s Create backend RAP Custom Entity - ZCE_RecentOrders for the first Table card for our overview page. In this card the grouping Analytics happens upon Customer, and it will work on Region Filter as well. 

Note:

  • Before going further let’s understand how this works. As mentioned earlier, overview page is for Embedded Analytics calculation on different metrics. so, if Region is passed in filter, then from the custom entity class first the overall table will be filtered with Region and then the grouping metrics along with aggregation data lone will be taken in another select query and grouped for output. 
  • We are giving Annotation in each custom entity as the same direction path will be configured in Front end Manifest which will reflect in the overview card. 
@EndUserText.label: 'Recent Orders for Table Card'
@ObjectModel.query.implementedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_OVP_RAP_QUERY'
define root custom entity ZCE_RecentOrders
{
      @UI.lineItem: [{ position: 10, label: 'Customer' }]
  key Customer    : abap.char(40);

      @UI.lineItem: [{ position: 20, label: 'Status' }]
      Status      : abap.char(10);

      @UI.hidden  : true
      StatusColor : abap.int1;

      @UI.lineItem: [{ position: 30, label: 'Amount' }]
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'Currency'
      OrderAmount : abap.curr(15,2);
      @UI.hidden  : true
      Currency    : abap.cuky;
      @UI.hidden  : true
      Region        : abap.char(20);
}

4. The custom entity - ZCE_SALES_BAR_LIST_CARD is created for second card and this will be configured as Bar list card template in Overview page. This card has calculative metrics on source and target and for that datapoint annotations were also done. 

@ObjectModel.query.implementedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_OVP_RAP_QUERY'
@EndUserText.label: 'Sales Bar Chart List'
define root custom entity ZCE_SALES_BAR_LISTCARD
{
      @UI.dataPoint: { title: 'Order Count', description: 'Total Orders', qualifier: 'CountKPI' }
  key TotalOrders : abap.int4;
      @UI.lineItem: [{ position: 10, label: 'Status' }]
  key status: abap.char(20);
      @UI.lineItem: [{ position: 20, type: #AS_DATAPOINT }]
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'Currency'
      @UI.dataPoint: {
       visualization: #PROGRESS,
       targetValue: 1000,
      criticality: 'StatusColor'
      }
      TotalSales : abap.curr(15,2);
      @UI.lineItem: [{ position: 30, type: #AS_DATAPOINT,  label: 'Target Value' }]
      @UI.dataPoint: {
         title: 'Target'
       }
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'Currency'
      TargetSales : abap.curr(15,2);
      StatusColor : abap.int1;
      Currency    : abap.cuky;
       @UI.hidden  : true
      Region        : abap.char(20);
}

5. The custom entity - ZCE_FINAL_DIV_TAX is created for third card and this will be configured as list card template in Overview page. In this card the grouping happens on Division and in addition it will have KPI Header which will give overall Tax Average for the respective filter. Datapoint Annotation is configured for KPI header. 

@ObjectModel.query.implementedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_OVP_RAP_QUERY'
@EndUserText.label: 'Tax Average'
define root custom entity ZCE_FINAL_DIV_TAX
{
      @UI.lineItem: [{ position: 10, label: 'Division' }]
  key Division : abap.char(5);
       @UI.lineItem: [{ position: 20, label: 'Tax %' }]
      TaxAverage : abap.dec(15,2);
      @UI.dataPoint: { qualifier: 'SalesData', 
                       title: 'Tax Average',
                       criticality: 'StatusColor', 
                       valueFormat.numberOfFractionalDigits: 2 }
      GlobalTaxAverage : abap.dec(15,2);
      StatusColor : int1;
       @UI.hidden  : true
      Region        : abap.char(20);
}

 6. Now lets create CDS View Entity - ZCDS_REGION_ORDERS instead of RAP Custom Entity for Region card which will be configured as 4th overview card with Table card template. 

Why CDS View for this card ? - Because this card has property Region which will work directly once the Overview filter Region is entered. URL will intrepret $filter Region to this CDS view and since this view has Region Property built within itself and henceforth it can be filtered easily. 

Note: Instead of custom Entity, we can also use CDS with parameters in backend but that will support only if the CDS View is Analytical CDS. But unfortunately RAP V2 Does not support Analytical CDS View. CDS with Analytical is supported in RAP with OData V4, but again unfortunately OData V4 is not supported in BTP Overview page template as of today. So, these are the challenges I faced and to avoid more complications I went with custom entity for the other cards. 

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Orders Delivered by Region'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: false
define root view entity ZCDS_REGION_ORDERS
as select from zovp_analytics
{
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 10, label: 'Region' }]
  key region     as Region,
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 20, label: 'Orders Delivered' }]
  sum(order_del) as OrdersDelivered,
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 30, label: 'Order Amount' }]
  @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'Currency'
  sum(totalamount) as OrderAmount,
  currency
} group by region, currency;

7. Till now we created Custom Entities for Table and list card. For KPI card, we can create Custom Entity now. In this card Total sales happened for all region would be collectively calculated.

Note: Since this is KPI card, from front end we may choose Analytical card template to show KPI data alone and from backend we must have selection variant and data point annotations. 

@ObjectModel.query.implementedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_OVP_RAP_QUERY'
@EndUserText.label: 'Region KPI'
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true
@UI.presentationVariant: [{ qualifier: 'KPIRegion', 
                            text: 'KPI: Total Region Sales', 
                            visualizations: [{type: #AS_DATAPOINT, 
                                              element:  'TotalAmount',
                                              qualifier: 'TotalAmount' }] }]
@UI.selectionVariant: [{ qualifier: 'KPIRegion', 
                         text: 'KPI: Total Region Sales'  }]
@UI.selectionPresentationVariant: [{ qualifier: 'KPIRegion',
                                   presentationVariantQualifier: 'KPIRegion',
